n_seagrass: Halophila ovalis and Enhalus acoroides form the meadow floor of Indo-Pacific coastal wetlands, fixing carbon at rates rivalling terrestrial forests through dense rhizome mats that anchor sediment and oxygenate root zones. Globally, seagrass meadows have declined by 29% since 1879, with Southeast Asian beds losing an estimated 30,000 km² to turbidity and eutrophication driven by coastal agriculture.
n_shrimp: Penaeus monodon — the giant tiger prawn — navigates between mangrove nursery and open estuary across its life cycle, converting detritus and algae into protein that supports every predator above it in the column. Thailand and Vietnam have converted over 2.5 million hectares of mangrove to shrimp aquaculture ponds since 1980, hollowing out the very nursery habitat the wild population depends on.
n_heron: Mycteria leucocephala, the Painted Stork, feeds by tactile bill-sweeping through shallow water — a foraging style that requires clear, fish-dense wetlands it can locate by sight from kilometers away. Colony counts at Bharatpur and across peninsular Thailand have dropped roughly 30% in two decades as wetland fish biomass collapses under eutrophication; when prey density falls below threshold, pairs simply abandon nesting attempts for the season.
n_runoff: Nitrogen and phosphorus loading from paddy fields and aquaculture effluent arrives as dissolved inorganic nutrients, triggering phytoplankton and epiphyte blooms that smother seagrass leaves and crash dissolved oxygen to near-anoxic levels at night. The Mekong Delta alone exports an estimated 450,000 tonnes of reactive nitrogen to coastal waters annually — enough to sustain persistent hypoxic zones that silence the benthic community for weeks at a time.
n_crab: Scylla serrata — the giant mud crab — is a burrowing omnivore of the mangrove floor that grazes seagrass rhizomes and detritus in the same beds the shrimp depend on. Where nursery habitat shrinks, crab and shrimp collide over a shrinking food base: a booming crab population can graze a seagrass meadow faster than it regrows, quietly starving the shrimp even when the water runs clear. The lever is the meadow itself — restore the seagrass and it feeds both; let it thin and the two competitors crash together.
Mozambique Tilapia: Oreochromis mossambicus — the Mozambique tilapia — is an IUCN '100 worst' invader that tolerates brackish water from fresh to near-marine, breeds year-round, and mouth-broods its young through conditions that wipe out native fry. Escapees from aquaculture now dominate estuaries across Southeast Asia, where they strip benthic algae, prey on juvenile shrimp and crustaceans, and out-compete native species for nursery habitat — a single pair can found a population that crowds out the food web within a season. Removal, not poison, is the only lever: sustained culling drives the colony below the density it needs to rebound.
Lionfish: Pterois volitans — the red lionfish — is an Indo-Pacific predator turned notorious invader: venomous, fearless, and hunting reef fish that never evolved to avoid it. A lionfish can cut local prey populations by most of their number within weeks, and with no native predator to check it, a reef can flip from fish-rich to barren fast. The only lever is removal — sustained culling keeps its numbers below the threshold where it rebounds.
n_coral: Reef-building corals are animal-algae partnerships that lay down the entire three-dimensional habitat a reef depends on. Sediment and nutrient plumes smother them, blocking the light their symbionts need; once the living veneer dies, the structure erodes and every species above it loses its home. Clear the water and the coral regrows — slowly, but it is the foundation everything else is built on.
n_parrotfish: Parrotfish graze algae off the reef with beak-like teeth, keeping fast-growing turf from overrunning the coral — and grinding dead rock into the white sand of the lagoon. As mid-web grazers they feed the reef's predators while protecting the coral itself; lose them and algae smothers the reef from a second direction.
n_urchin: Sea urchins graze the same reef algae as the parrotfish — useful in balance, ruinous in excess. When their numbers boom they strip the substrate bare, scouring away the coral recruits and the food the parrotfish need, turning a living reef into bare rock. The meadow of algae is the shared table; restore the coral and it feeds both grazers.
n_shark: The blacktip reef shark is the reef's apex patrol — its predation keeps mid-level fish in check and the whole web in balance. Sharks are slow to mature and quick to disappear under fishing pressure; when the top predator goes, prey populations swing wildly and the reef destabilises from the top down.
n_sediment: Sediment plumes — from dredging, coastal clearing, and runoff — turn clear reef water turbid, blocking the sunlight corals need and settling as a smothering blanket over the living surface. Unlike a nutrient spike, sediment kills by shade and burial; cut it off at the source and the water clears from there outward.
